title: Paper inclusions, flowers and leaves
subtitle: Embedding botanicals in a hand-made sheet
excerpt: Plant material, dried flowers, leaves, grasses, petals, can be embedded in a hand-made sheet while the pulp is still on the mould. The inclusions become part of the sheet structure as the fibres bond around them during pressing and drying. The result is a translucent decorative paper useful for lampshades, greeting cards, and pressed-flower archives.
sourceNotes: Paper-inclusion techniques synthesised from the botanical papermaking tradition; cross-referenced with Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), public domain, which documents the historical use of plant material in Japanese and European papers. The modern craft technique is widely practised and not attributed to a single historical source.

glossaryTerms.definition: Transferring a freshly pulled sheet from the mould onto a board or felt for pressing and drying.
The feathered, untrimmed edge of a hand-formed sheet, where the pulp crept slightly under the deckle frame.

projectSchedule.title: Pull base sheet, apply inclusions, add covering layer
projectSchedule.body: Pull the base sheet. Lay botanicals on the wet sheet. Apply a thin second sheet or dilute pulp over the top.

projectSchedule.title: Press and dry
projectSchedule.body: Press overnight then peel and dry for two days.

content.text: Inclusions work because the wet pulp fibres bond around the plant material during pressing and drying, locking it in position. Thin, flat botanicals, dried flower petals, leaves pressed flat, fronds of grass or fern, embed most successfully; thick materials (dried berries, seed pods, thick stem sections) create uneven pressure during pressing and may pop out of a thin sheet. The key choice is between light-coloured pulp (which lets the botanicals read as inclusions with clear borders) and a heavier pulp coverage (which buries the inclusions in the paper and makes them visible only in transmitted light).

attrs.title: Blender safety
attrs.body: Keep the blender unplugged when loading or rinsing, and do not blend botanicals with thick stems or seeds.

items.name: Cotton-linter or recycled-paper pulp, prepared and in the vat
Dried botanicals, flower petals, small leaves, grass blades, short thread scraps
items.qty: A small amount: 5 to 10 items per sheet

content.text: Pull a standard base sheet from the vat. While the sheet is still on the mould and the surface is wet, lay the botanicals gently on the upper face of the wet pulp. Press each one flat with a clean finger. Apply a thin covering layer by pouring a small cup of diluted pulp over the mould and tilting it to spread the pulp evenly over the botanicals. This covering layer bonds the inclusions in from above; the base layer bonds from below.
Drain the excess water by tilting the mould.
the sheet onto a board and press through the mesh firmly. The
will be irregular where the covering layer overflowed, this is part of the character of inclusion sheets.

Inclusion sheets need longer pressing than plain sheets because the botanical material holds water. Press for two hours under maximum weight before peeling. Air-dry flat, do not hang, as the sheet weight may distort along the inclusion edges.

items.symptom: The botanicals fall off the sheet when the mould is lifted after couching
items.cause: The covering layer was not applied, or was too thin to bond the inclusions in place from above.
items.fix: Apply the covering layer of diluted pulp directly over the botanicals before couching. Tilt the mould gently to spread it; do not pour too fast or the botanicals will shift.

items.symptom: The sheet tears along the edge of a leaf inclusion
items.cause: The leaf is too thick relative to the surrounding sheet, creating a stress line at its edge.
items.fix: Choose the thinnest available botanicals. Press flowers and leaves in a flower press for two weeks before use, this reduces their thickness substantially.
